content.full_text:
November 12, 1976


Honorable Elwood Lui, Judge
Municipal Court
Los Angeles Judicial District
Division 33, Room 3-317
210 West Temple Street
Los Angeles, California 90012

Dear Judge Lui:

In response to your letter of October 20, and our subsequent
conversation, attached is an information sheet which was sent
to all county election officials, clerks of the court and
administrative and executive officers of the court on
November 10, 1976.

I trust this information will answer any questions you may have.
If, however, you desire additional assistance, please do not
hesitate to let me know.

Very truly yours,


REGINA SICILIANO-KUTCHINS
Technical Assistance Program Coordinator


RSK:mak
Attachment

bcc: Jeanne P.

FPPC
Oct 22 9:37 AM'76

IN CHAMBERS

MUNICIPAL COURT
LOS ANGELES JUDICIAL DISTRICT
ELWOOD LUI, JUDGE

TELEPHONE
(213) 974-6111

October 20, 1976

Fair Political Practices Commission
State of California
P. O. Box 307
Sacramento, California 95804

Attention: Executive/Legal Division

Gentlemen:

Shortly, a number of judges of our
Court will be required to file Candidates Campaign
Statements Short Form Form 470 on the basis that
no more than $200 was received or spent during
the candidate's filing period.

One of the questions on this particular
form is the residence address and telephone number
of the individual judge. I, and a number of
other judges of this Court, strenuously object
to including our residential addresses on these
publicly filed documents. The security reasons
for such objections are obvious.

I would like to know your comments
regarding our objections and what action you
would take if the information was refused or
left blank.

Your early response would be appreciated.

Very truly yours,

EL;lf                              Elwood Lui

cc: Com. Jerrold E. Mitchell

I, and a number of other Judges of this Court, strenuously object to including our residential addresses on these publicly filed documents. The security reasons for such objections are obvious. I would like to know your comments regarding our objections and what action you would take if the information was refused or left blank.
sections.question_synthetic: Must judges filing candidate campaign statements on Form 1-70 include their residential addresses and telephone numbers despite security concerns?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC provided an information sheet previously sent to county election officials and court officers on November 10, 1976, in response to the judge's concerns about residential address disclosure requirements, though the specific conclusion is not included in the available document text.

I, and a number of other Judges of this Court, strenuously object to including our residential addresses on these publicly filed documents. The security reasons for such objections are obvious. I would like to know your comments regarding our objections and what action you would take if the information was refused or left blank.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC provided an information sheet previously sent to county election officials and court officers on November 10, 1976, in response to the judge's concerns about residential address disclosure requirements, though the specific conclusion is not included in the available document text.

embedding.summary: Judge Elwood Lui requested FPPC guidance on whether judges must disclose residential addresses and phone numbers on publicly filed candidate campaign statements (Form 1-70), citing security concerns. The FPPC responded by referencing an information sheet distributed to election officials addressing such disclosure requirements.
